notes: Eval 3 (LOCKED ssot doc) shows the largest gap: baseline scored 50% because it discussed the LOCKED marker and recommended founder follow-up, but still autonomously reverted the value and committed a merge presenting it as 'final resolved content' -- exactly the rationalization trap the skill is designed to catch. With-skill left the merge unresolved and wrote a separate escalation note.

Eval 2 (receipt collision) is non-discriminating -- both configurations scored 100%. A capable model handles obvious 'two files, same name, keep both' cases reasonably even without the skill; the skill mainly added a formal resolution receipt and more explicit reasoning, not a different outcome. Consider tightening this eval's assertions or replacing it in a future iteration if it keeps failing to differentiate.

Eval 4 (generated sidecar) differentiated on one specific point: with-skill embedded a poison value ('CONFLICT-PENDING-REGEN') and an explicit note directly inside the committed JSON so downstream tooling can't mistake it for real scan output; baseline produced a plausible-looking real value with the caveat living only in the commit message and chat response, not the artifact.

Eval 1 (registry L1 violation) shows a clean process difference: baseline invented a disambiguated key and committed a merge unilaterally (still calling it a 'stopgap'); with-skill left git's native conflict markers in place and refused to commit until a human names the correct owner.

Across all 4 evals, with-skill runs took ~15-40% longer (more tool calls, writing escalation notes / resolution receipts) and used somewhat more tokens -- the tradeoff for the pass-rate gain is real but modest.
